title: Thrysos, King of Revels
subtitle: Ecstasy & Excess

lore.description: Where torches flare and cups spill, where dancers stamp until dawn, there strides Thrysos crowned with ivy and horn. He is both host and beast, a patron of license who laughs at law. In his train follow the masked and the wild, the broken who dance to forget, the bold who revel to remember, and the lustful who seek the heat of bodies in the dark. His rites intoxicate, inflame, and unmask — yet each cup leaves a mark, each kiss a chain, each night of abandon a dawn of reckoning.
lore.followers: Thrysos rules the spectrum from the grinning drunkard who sings bawdy songs in taverns to the ecstatic cults whose rites strip away not only clothes but every pretense of civilization. He is the god of the Bacchanalia, the wild hunt of the senses, the moment when the watcher becomes the participant and the participant becomes the sacrifice. In his milder aspect, he blesses the fertility of the harvest and the laughter of lovers. In his darker aspect, he drives maenads to tear apart the hero who refused to join the dance.

names_across_regions.linn: The Storm-Dancers, who whirl through gales until exhaustion, their cries mistaken for ecstasy
names_across_regions.aelinnel: The Unbounded Guest, whose arrival at a feast is marked by laughter, shattered glass, and at least one scandalous elopement
names_across_regions.zakov: The Revel-King, invoked before every pirate carouse, with the understanding that a hungover crew is a vulnerable one — and that the captain's bed is never empty
names_across_regions.vhasia: Whispered by those who seek a potion to lower inhibitions or a curse of unquenchable desire

signs: A mask that smiles without a wearer
The sudden warmth of a stranger's hand on your arm
The scent of myrrh and sweat clinging to a garment never worn
lore.quote: Drink, and be unafraid. Kiss, and be unbound. Dance, and be devoured. Every mask is true when worn long enough, and every truth is a mask for hunger.

domain_focus: Ecstatic Release: dance, intoxication, the shedding of inhibition (and clothing)
Masked Freedom: identity as performance, truth through disguise, the thrill of the anonymous encounter
Feast and Famine: excess, appetite, lust, and the cost of indulgence
Ritual Frenzy: communal ecstasy that transforms participants into something feral and beautiful
The Bacchic Dark: the point where pleasure tips into violence, where the maenad tears apart the intruder

thiasos.description: A Runekeeper sworn to Thrysos does not bind a familiar of quiet habits. Their Thiasos is a small beast or being of primal appetites: a satyr-kid with too-bright eyes that nibbles at wine-soaked bread, a fox that dances on its hind legs when the moon is full, a ferret that steals silk ribbons from lovers' bedposts, a gecko that clings to wine jugs and changes color with desire, or a small bronze cymbal that claps of its own accord during moments of flirtation.
thiasos.care: This creature requires no ordinary food, but it must be present during an act of indulgence (drinking, dancing, lovemaking, or feasting) each day. If neglected for a week, it becomes Compromised (it shrieks at inappropriate moments, or its antics become grotesque, imposing –1 die on all social rolls involving subtlety or discretion for its bearer).

codex.description: The Codex of Thrysos is never a sober book. It may be a wine-stained journal of erotic poetry, a set of ivory tablets carved with scenes of revelry, a leather folio containing pressed flowers from orgies, or a scroll that unravels further each time the reader laughs.
codex.upkeep: Because Thrysos's truths are written in the body's language, the Codex requires upkeep: the Runekeeper must spend an hour each week adding a new verse, sketch, or memory of indulgence, or the Rites stored there become dull (treat as Compromised until reinvigorated with a night of celebration).

patrons_gift.name: Thrysos's Blessing
patrons_gift.description: Once per scene as an action, a Runekeeper of Thrysos may touch a held item (weapon, tool, or garment) and whisper a word of desire.
patrons_gift.effect: For the rest of the scene, the item grants +1 die to Performance or Sway rolls involving seduction, charm, or intimidation, and the first time each scene the bearer would be affected by a Fear effect, they may laugh it off (ignore it).
patrons_gift.cost: After the scene, the user marks +1 Obligation to Thrysos.

rites.name: Rite of the Overflowing Cup
rites.materials: A brimming vessel of wine (or a more intimate offering).
rites.effect: Allies who drink gain +1 die to social rolls this scene; enemies who drink suffer –1 die. Those who drink deeply may find their inhibitions loosened in other ways (GM's discretion for romantic or scandalous complications).
rites.push_it: All in Near must test Resolve (DV 3) or be swept into revelry, possibly leading to unplanned liaisons or public confessions; mark 1 SB (Hearts). Cost: Mark +1 Obligation (total +2) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

rites.name: Rite of the Horned Dance
rites.materials: A mask or antlers (carved from a stag that died in rut).
rites.effect: Enter an ecstatic frenzy: +2 dice to melee or performance; ignore 1 Fatigue per round. Your movements become both seductive and threatening; those watching may be captivated or disturbed.
rites.push_it: The frenzy spreads: one ally joins you in the dance, but both mark 1 SB (Spades) as the lines between combat and carnality blur. Cost: Mark +1 Obligation (total +2) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

rites.name: Rite of the Breaking Mask
rites.materials: A mask smashed in hand (or a lover's token torn in two).
rites.effect: Reveal the true face beneath the social mask: the target suffers –2 dice to disguise, deceit, or composure until scene end. They may also be compelled to admit a hidden desire (GM's discretion).
rites.push_it: Truth is forced: the target must declare one hidden desire aloud, possibly to the wrong person; mark 1 SB (Diamonds). Cost: Mark +1 Obligation (total +2) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

rites.name: Rite of the Wild Procession
rites.materials: A horn sounded thrice (or a kiss passed around a circle).
rites.effect: Summon spectral revelers — satyrs, dancers, horned riders, and a few figures whose embraces are more than friendly. For the scene, the zone is [WARD]ed: hostile entry forces 1 SB (Spades); allies gain +1 die to resist compulsion or fear. The revelers may also grant a boon of Vigor to one couple (restore 1 Fatigue to two allies who share a bond of intimacy).
rites.push_it: The procession spills beyond the zone; outsiders in Near must test Resolve (DV 4) or join the dance — and perhaps the revel's other activities. Cost: Mark +1 Obligation (total +2) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

rites.name: Rite of the Endless Feast
rites.materials: A feast laid for twelve, with wine, music, and comfortable cushions.
rites.effect: For one scene, the zone floods with plenty: allies restore Fatigue; all rolls tied to appetite or indulgence gain +2 dice. Leaving requires Resolve (DV 3) — the comforts are hard to abandon. The feast also grants a temporary Heightened Desire condition: the first seduction or intimidation roll each ally makes gains +1 die.
rites.push_it: The feast persists as a hunger-haunt; start Hedonist's Curse [6]. Participants may find themselves unable to derive pleasure from mundane means, always craving the excess of that night. Cost: Mark +1 Obligation (total +3) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

rites.name: Rite of the Reveler's Apotheosis
rites.materials: A crown of twisted vines that bleed when touched, the first laugh of a newborn, a promise broken at a crossroads, and the willing touch of a lover.
rites.effect: Grant the ultimate reveler's gift — transformation into pure ecstatic essence. The target becomes immune to fear and despair, gains +3 dice to performance or frenzy, and can inspire allies within Near (+1 die to all rolls). Their aura also carries a scent of desire; anyone within Near who sees them must test Resolve (DV 3) or be mildly infatuated (disadvantage on hostile actions).
rites.push_it: The transformation becomes contagious — nearby allies may also transform but risk losing their humanity (their sense of self blurs into the ecstatic mob); mark +2 Obligation. Cost: Mark +1 Obligation (total +4) or Mark +1 Fatigue.

cantors.name: The Cantor's Song
cantors.description: A Cantor of Thrysos sings in off-key harmonies — melodies that start wild and grow wilder, rhythms that mimic a galloping heart. Their voice is used to whip a crowd into a frenzy, to calm a hangover with a lullaby, or to make a mask cling to a face until the wearer is ready to take it off. Thrysos's Cantors are often found at festivals, in taverns at closing time, leading the procession of the horned dancers, or performing in private salons where the line between audience and participant is deliberately blurred.

cult.name: The Cult of the Horned Circle
cult.description: Thrysos's cults meet in clearings, barns, and the back rooms of inns after midnight. The Horned Circle is a fellowship of mask-carvers, vintners, musicians, courtesans, and those who have tasted the edge of ecstasy — sometimes all in the same person. Their most sacred vessel is a hollowed-out log that was struck by lightning; they ferment wine inside it, and the wine whispers secrets to those who drink it — forgotten grudges, hidden desires, the true names of those who will betray them, and the faces of those who are willing. A ritual of the Circle involves each member donning a mask (hand-carved, never bought) and dancing until the first dancer falls from exhaustion. The fallen is crowned with ivy and declared 'guest of honor' — they lead the next circle, and sometimes the next bed. Their creed is carved into a single wine barrel that is never emptied: 'The cup that never empties is the cup you share.' Outsiders are welcome to dance, but they must leave their mask behind when they go — and if they drink from the lightning-log, they will hear the whispers for a year and a day, and they will return.

witchcraft.description: A witch who walks with Thrysos is a mask-carver or wine-smith — one who crafts the tools of ecstatic release: a mask that lets the wearer dance without tiring, a drum that beats in time with the drinker's pulse, a cup that never runs dry (but leaves a headache at dawn), or a perfume that makes the wearer irresistible for a single night. Their magic works through the materials of celebration: a thyrsus that marks the boundary of a sacred dance, a press that squeezes wine that ferments in hours instead of weeks, a needle that sews a costume that makes the wearer faster, louder, bolder, or a small clay lamp that burns with a flame that kindles desire. In Aeler, a True-Mason might carve a 'revel keystone' into a feast hall, but a hedge-witch of Thrysos might instead drive a thyrsus into the floorboards to consecrate the space for an orgy.

thyrsus.name: The Thyrsus of the Unbound
thyrsus.description: A staff of fennel or oak, topped with a pine cone and bound with ivy, often with a ribbon from a lover's garment. Once per session, the witch may strike the thyrsus against the ground three times. For the next scene, any creature within Near who is engaged in revelry (dancing, drinking, performing, or any act of intimacy) gains +1 die to social and physical actions, but any attempt to leave the zone requires a Resolve test (DV 3) — the revel holds them. The thyrsus can also be used to draw a circle on the ground; any mask placed within the circle becomes 'true' for one night — the wearer cannot be recognized by anyone who was not present at the drawing, and their inhibitions are lowered (they may act on desires they would normally suppress).

hedge_gifts.name: Dancer's Cramp
hedge_gifts.description: Once per scene, the witch may snap their fingers and whisper a dancer's rhythm. One target within Near range feels an irresistible urge to move — they must tap a foot, sway, or take a step. This cannot force them into danger, but it can ruin stealth, break a staredown, or distract a guard for one exchange. The target may resist with Resolve (DV 3) to ignore the urge. A more salacious version (Lover's Cramp) might be used in seduction scenes, forcing an unintended compliment or touch.

corruption.benefit: Reveler's Grace: +1 die to social rolls in feasts or crowds.
corruption.cost: Compulsive Toast: Must drink or mark 1 Fatigue when drink is offered.

corruption.benefit: Horned Vitality: Ignore the first 1 Fatigue each scene.
corruption.cost: Restless Appetite: –1 die when abstaining from indulgence (food, drink, or touch).

corruption.benefit: Revel-Kin: Once/scene, summon a minor revel-spirit (advantage die) — a satyr, a maenad, or a muse of seduction.
corruption.cost: Masked Self: Cannot act without a role or guise; –1 die to plain sincerity.

corruption.benefit: Bacchic Fury: Once/session, frenzy grants +2 dice to melee for one exchange. Also, once per scene, ignore the effects of a single social faux pas (the revel covers it).
corruption.cost: Ecstatic Blindness: –1 die to perception outside the revel; you may not notice threats that require sobriety.

corruption.benefit: Feast Without End: Once/session, allies in Near clear all Fatigue.
corruption.cost: Hungering Host: You consume twice as much; denial causes Harm 1 (Stress). You may also develop a reputation: every feast you attend ends in scandal.

corruption.benefit: Satyr King's Mantle: For one scene, become an avatar of revels; all allies gain +2 dice, enemies suffer –2 dice. Your presence also inspires desire: once during the scene, compel one target to act on a hidden passion (Resolve DV 4).
corruption.cost: Tyrant of Pleasure: Mark +3 Obligation; the revel grows monstrous, demanding a sacrifice (an oath, a relationship, or a life). Beast of Endless Revel: Permanent transformation — your mortal form becomes optional; must make Presence+Performance (DV 4) to appear civilized or risk terrifying (or entrancing) all who see you into joining your eternal celebration. You are always at least mildly intoxicated, and sobriety causes you physical pain (1 Fatigue per hour).

playstyle_notes.description: Thrysos rewards those who embrace risk, abandon convention, and find power in letting go — both the laughter of the drunkard and the ecstasy of the maenad. His followers become masters of ecstatic ritual, social manipulation through celebration and seduction, and the dangerous art of losing control. The corruption progression leads toward a beast of endless revel — powerful, charismatic, but never truly sober, never truly at rest, and always hungry. Ideal for players who enjoy wild scenes, social chaos, characters who dance on the edge of self-destruction, and those who wish to explore the full spectrum of hedonism from playful flirtation to violent frenzy.
emphasizes: Ecstatic Release: music, dance, intoxication, the shedding of inhibition (and clothing)
Masked Freedom: identity as performance, the thrill of the anonymous encounter
Feast and Famine: excess, appetite, lust, and the cost of indulgence
Ritual Frenzy: communal ecstasy that transforms participants into something feral and beautiful
The Bacchic Dark: the point where pleasure tips into violence, where the revel demands blood
Seduction as Weapon: using desire to manipulate, distract, or destroy

monastic_tradition.name: Drunken Master (Thrysos)
monastic_tradition.quote: The true disciple does not fight the wine — they let the wine fight for them. Every stagger is a feint, every spill a trip, every belch a battle cry.
monastic_tradition.prerequisites: Body 3+, Spirit 3+, Rite of the Horned Dance or Cantor of Thrysos.
monastic_tradition.debt_resistant_frame: Thrysos's monks owe nothing but the next glass. Monks of the Drunken Master tradition gain +1 die to ignore or resist any magical debt, geas, or contract that would compel them to act against their revelry. This boon can be used once per scene, but each use leaves a faint scent of wine on the monk — the Hollow may find them easier to track (GM gains 1 SB). If they succeed, the creditor's demand slides off like water off a drunkard's back.

techniques.name: Stagger Step
techniques.effect: When an enemy misses you with a melee attack, move one band (Close↔Near) without provoking opportunity attacks.

techniques.name: Spill the Cup
techniques.effect: As a reaction to being grappled, slip free and impose –1 die on the attacker's next roll.

techniques.name: Drunken Fist
techniques.effect: Unarmed strikes gain +1 die and ignore 1 Armor when you are at Fatigue 2+.

techniques.name: Hangover Resilience
techniques.effect: Once per scene, after a complication from intoxication, clear 1 Fatigue.

techniques.name: Satyr's Sprawl
techniques.effect: Immune to being knocked prone while moving; standing from prone is a free action.

techniques.name: Bacchic Counter
techniques.effect: When you succeed on a Desperate defense roll, make a free unarmed attack against the attacker.

master_technique.name: The Unbroken Bottle
master_technique.description: Once per session, ignore all Fatigue penalties for one scene. When you would be hit by a melee attack, you may force the attacker to hit another adjacent creature of your choice. After the scene, you suffer Harm 2 (Exhaustion). You gain a permanent Debt Scar — a purple bruise shaped like a grape cluster — but may ignore one forced Obligation or debt repayment that targets you (the patron's mark confuses the creditor).

sample_adventure.title: The Revel That Never Ends
sample_adventure.description: A village on the edge of the Mistlands has been celebrating the same harvest festival for three weeks. No one can leave; no one wants to leave. The wine flows without end, the music never stops, and the dancers have begun to forget their own names. The village elder, sober and terrified, begs the party to find the source of the revelry — a mask that was carved from a lightning-struck tree, now worn by the village's most beautiful and most dangerous inhabitant. The mask is Thrysos's gift, and it will not come off until the wearer has danced their way to ecstatic oblivion. The party must break the revel, convince the wearer to remove the mask, or find a way to end the celebration without being swept into the dance themselves. But the music is beautiful, and the wine is very, very good.
sample_adventure.quote: The mask that dances without a wearer, the wine that whispers secrets, the revel that does not end — these are Thrysos's gifts, and they are never free. The price is always a piece of yourself, given willingly. And sometimes, the price is everything.
